macro_segments.title: The debate between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Yose regarding the dimensions of the copper altar and the sanctification of the courtyard in Solomon's time

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The blood requires the altar, but the ketores does not
Rabbi Yehuda's reading: the altar was wide and low (10x10x3), allowing sanctification of the courtyard floor
Rabbi Yose's reading: the altar was narrow and tall (5x5x10), requiring higher curtains for privacy
Resolving the contradiction: privacy through hidden handiwork despite visible priest
Connecting the altar debate to Solomon's sanctification of the courtyard
Two interpretations of 'he sanctified the courtyard': functional altar versus preparation for new altar
The hermeneutical basis for the dispute: connecting outer to outer versus vessel to vessel

macro_segments.title: The exclusive requirement of an altar for blood service versus entrails and incense

micro_segments.title: Rava's statement: Rabbi Yehuda concedes that the blood work absolutely requires an altar
The Paschal lamb blood mixture proof: gathering spilled blood to sprinkle on the altar
Refutation of the floor-as-altar theory: the practical need to gather and carry blood to the altar
Alternative explanation: intentional human agency versus accidental spillage
Tosafot's position: an altar is obviously required for blood; the novelty is that entrails and incense do not require it
The theological theme: blood as the focus of the korban, not the burning of entrails

macro_segments.title: The invalidation of a chipped altar and the requirement for a whole altar during shechita

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar: a chipped altar prevents both sprinkling and shechita of the korban
Extension to mincha: the remnant cannot be eaten when the altar is chipped
Hermeneutical basis: the verse 'beside the altar' links mincha consumption to the altar's wholeness
Leap to kodshei kodashim: the eating of meat also requires a whole altar

macro_segments.title: Extension to kodshim kalim: even outside the Temple, eating requires a whole altar

micro_segments.title: Scenario: kodshim kalim eaten in Jerusalem but altar is chipped elsewhere—can one continue eating?
Abaye's source: Rabbi Ishmael's principle that ma'aser sheni requires the presence of the Temple
Rabbi Ishmael's teaching: ma'aser sheni should be eaten in Jerusalem in modern times despite lack of Temple
Reasoning: ma'aser sheni is linked to bechor by the Torah verse; both require Temple
Debate: whether the link is to the Temple structure or to the sanctity of the place

macro_segments.title: The case of bechor meat after Temple destruction: linking flesh to blood

micro_segments.title: Hypothetical: bechor blood thrown before destruction, meat eaten after—is it permitted?

macro_segments.title: The case of bechor meat after Temple destruction: linking flesh to blood (continued)

micro_segments.title: Connection: bechor flesh is linked to bechor blood by the Torah verse
Conclusion: meat consumption requires the same altar-presence as blood sprinkling

macro_segments.title: The invalidation of a chipped altar and the requirement for a whole altar during shechita (continued)

micro_segments.title: The verse linking emurim, blood, and flesh establishes that meat eating parallels blood work

macro_segments.title: Resolving the double hekesh: meat and blood are one unit, not two separate inferences

micro_segments.title: Objection: 'A matter learned by hekesh does not return to teach by hekesh'
Solution: meat and blood are a single entity, making the connection direct rather than double inference

macro_segments.title: The camp in motion: when the altar moves, meat of kodshei kodashim cannot be eaten

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiya's critique of Babylonian scholars and the teaching about the moving camp
Baraita: when the camp moves, kodashim cannot be eaten, but zavim are still separated from camp
Resolution: kodshei kodashim require the standing mizbeach; kodshim kalim do not
Parallel to ma'aser sheni: eating in Jerusalem does not require the mizbeach to be standing

macro_segments.title: Alternative version: Rav Papa's dispute between Babylon and Eretz Israel on spilled blood

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa: disagreement arises only when blood is spilled, not when the mizbeach is destroyed
Babylonian position: eating requires that blood was sprinkled on the mizbeach
Eretz Israel position: eating is permitted once slaughter is valid, independent of blood sprinkling
